sentences.text: [SIGHS] We are coming now--
It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] November, 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are coming on to the year 1944.
Very little.
All what the Poles, bringing raw materials to the Madritsch factory were telling us.
In 1944, the Madritsch factory, which was, which was located outside the ghetto, and we were walking from the, from camp, into the Madritsch factory and back.
It was about three miles each-- each way.
The Madritsch factory was relocated into the camp, into the camp itself, the Plaszow, Plaszow camp.
At that time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Madritsch firm had an individual, by the name of Raimund Titsch.
Raimund Titsch, he is mentioned also in the Keneally book.
He somehow established a relationship with my father.
He had-- he was a nice man.
He was an Austrian, a Viennese.
And he had great admiration for my father's perfect knowledge of German and my father's intellectual ability.
So he somehow established with him a relationship of not friendship, but perhaps of some kind of admiration for the knowledge of my father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father took advantage of it, took fortunate advantage of it, by asking Titsch to intercede with Oskar Schindler, to take us into his [INAUDIBLE] factory.
The, once the camp, Plaszow camp is liquidated and it is being liquidated because the Russians were coming, closer, and closer, to Krakow.
I heard that Schindler was a fine German, a good German, who took good care of his Jews, who were working at that time outside.
It was like a branch of Plaszow, a branch not far from Plaszow, but about I think 2 and 1/2 miles from Plaszow.
It was his factory.
And the Jews were also quartered in the-- in the factory, in Schindler's factory.
So Titsch, knowing, having known Schindler well, interceded on our behalf, on behalf of my father and myself, that we should be included in his list.
I knew only that those Jews were well provided for by Schindler, who-- those who were, who were working for him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And thank god [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was successful.
We were included in the list of Schindler's-- of Schindler.

So that when the camp was liquidated in September 1944, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we went together with the people of the Emalia office of this factory, with one transport to the main concentration camp of Gross-Rosen in Germany.
We were transported in cattle cars.
No, no.
In Plaszow, I worked at Madritsch, where Titsch, Raimund Titsch was also the-- one of the-- owners, or one of the directors.
He was really a director, one of the directors, yes.
I think once or twice.
He visited the, the Plaszow camp.
And I saw-- I had, I got a glimpse of him.
No, because they were iso-- they were completely separated from us.
There we had-- there is no-- there were-- there was-- the only contact we had with them was through the Polish go, go-betweens, suppliers of raw material.
Possibly.
I cannot remember.
But I think I knew some of them, yes.
So--
There was always torture.
There was always torture.
There was not only-- chicanery and, and, and torture for torture's sake.
Like, for example we were-- after we worked a whole day, we were chased out from our barracks and made to carry big stones to one place, and then from that place back and deposit it where they were.
Just to, just to make, to make life miserable, to make life unbearable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that time, of course, killings were an everyday occurrence.
They were bringing people from outside, people who were on Aryan papers, who tried to, to get away.
And they were shot on a, on a hill there, in Plaszow.
The--you mean the hill?
It had a name.
But it was a dirty name.
I don't want to-- it's not dignified to say it.
It was a dirty name.
But everybody know the-- knew the hill.
And this was a special hill, where Ukrainian guards, together with Germans, were shooting people wholesale, every day.
We heard it.
We heard shots.
But the Germans in their ingenious ways always were hiding their crimes.
They tried, at least, to hide their crimes.
My uncle was shot.
My uncle was carrying a wheelbarrow with earth, just working for-- to, you know, in, in, in the camp.
And a German saw him and said, it's not enough what you are doing.
It's not enough dirt that you put at the wheel-- your wheelbarrow.
I think that you have lived enough.
Come.
And he shot him, right there on the spot.
No.
I was told about it, how he died.
My-- on my mother's-- my mother's brother.
In the-- in, in Plaszow?
You'll be surprised.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a Passover, 1944.
It was April, '44.
Passover in the ghetto-- in, in Plaszow.
Some, some Pole, I think, smuggled flour into the camp.
And lo and behold, somebody made a-- some kind of a, of an oven.
And they were baking matzahs.
There were people who stood outside, guarding.
And they somehow did it in a way that there was no trace of, of, of, of this activity, that there was no smoke or something.
They hid it in such a way that nobody could tell that there was matzah being baked.
And sure enough, I got a little piece of matzah on, on the first-- on the first day of-- on the first Seder night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as far as eating, we were eating vegetables and, and, and some potatoes.
They was brought especially for those who, who did not want to eat [NON-ENGLISH] on Pesach.
But it was not enough.
We were always hungry.
There was, there was always hunger.
There was always starvation, not enough of anything.
But this was a feature that I will never forget.
It was a-- it was a-- an experience, that in 1944, in a concentration camp, to make a matzah.
No, no.
It was so hidden, it was well-camouflaged that nobody found out.
Yom Kippur was-- it was in 1943 was Yom-- was Yom Kippur, '44 was, yes, '43 and '44.
It was still in, in, in Plaszow.
What can I tell you of Yom Kippur-- Rosh Hashanna and Yom Kippur?
They were just davening.
They were just praying and hoping and imploring Hashem should have mercy and not let the remainder of his people be exterminated, be destroyed.
I think there were a few.
And each one was trying-- was, was crowding on us.
I mean, there were just a few.
But most, most of those Jews knew how to-- the, the prayers book by memory, most of them.
No, no. no.
I had a pair of Tefillin in, in the Plaszow ghetto.
I mean, in the camp of Plaszow, I had a pair of Tefillin.
I don't know how.
But, but you see, when we came from the ghetto into, into the camp of Plaszow, we were not searched, bodily.
And I had the Tefillin somewhere on my body.
I, I transferred them to, to, to the camp.
But Tefillin was-- were taken away.
And everything was taken away at Gross-Rosen.
Excuse me, at Gross-Rosen.
I used the Tefillin in Plaszow everyday, everyday before, before going to work.
I got up 15 minutes or half an hour earlier, in order to be able to daven, to, to, to, to, to say my prayers.
Yes, some who did not have it, who could not smuggle it out or who, who lost.
I mean, people lost so many, so many things.
And who lost things, they borrowed.
They borrowed from one another, yes, yes.
No.
Having money in the camp was a capital offense, punishable by being shot on the spot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Having money, oh, my goodness.
Money?
No way, no.
Well, people would-- were trading for tobacco, you know.
There were people who were hooked on cigarettes, who, who valued cigarettes more than they valued food.
So they traded food for cigarettes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was such people.
And whatever [INAUDIBLE], I mean-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tobacco was smuggled by the Polish suppliers coming into the-- into the camp.

Because there were Polish suppliers coming to the camp, to provide the, the, the factory with raw material and taking away uniforms and other, other things from the camp, into, into the proper places where they were needed, or they were ordered.
Yes, yes, there were many rabbis in the camp, many rabbis, who gave me, personally, great encouragement.
They gave me hope.
They, they, they instilled me faith in God that nothing-- that not everything is lost, that, that God will not let his people be completely destroyed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He can punish his people.
He can [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] devastate his people but not totally ex-- exterminate them or destroy them, no.

They always held up historical events before me and, and taught me about Spanish Inquisition, about the history of, of Bogdan Chmielnicki, where so many thousands, hundreds of thousands of Jews were, were killed.
And, and still, we always arose from the ashes.
And we always-- Hashem always had mercy in such a way that the-- our people should we-- should maintain its eternity and endurance.
I was very inspired.
I was very inspired.
I remember them very distinctly.
They're rabbis.
These were, these were, these were real, genuine rabbis.
I know there were many.

But I can't remember their names besides this Levertov, this rabbi Levertov, besides-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, there was one rabbi [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the camp, who, who died a death of a saint in camp, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who was my ideal, who, whom I not only admired, but I saw him as my role model and my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] spiritual light.
His name was the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi.
His formal name was Klingberg, K-L-I-N-G-B-E-R-G, Klingberg.
He loved me very much.
He was a saint, a real saint.

And he gave me a blessing that I am sure, I am convinced that the blessing helped me in my being saved, in my achieving the excellence, the professional excellence in my calling, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and in my life, in the fact that I have lived up to now.
This was in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] November, 1942.
No, I'm sorry.
It was-- no, no.
It was 1943 in Plaszow.
It was either in March or April 1943.
When he was sick.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I came to him [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with a, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a token of appreciation, a token of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] reverence.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then he gave me the blessing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And shortly afterwards, he was put to death by a German of whom he asked, I just want to put on my tallis katan, my fringes, my tzitzit.
Just let me put it on, then you can do away with me.
And the German said, OK, so he died as a saint, as a Kadosh.
No, but I heard of it.
I heard of it.
And since then, whenever I pray certain prayers, I always have him in mind.
His name and his holy, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his holy person, which I remember, especially his face.
With his, with his beautiful, great side locks and beard and saintly face.
A face of an angel, an angelic person, angelic figure.
He said to me, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] I am telling you now that you are some of the world to come.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't at that time understand exactly what it meant.
But the future years, I remembered this blessing very well.
In the future years, I met his son.
And I shared this with this son.
And his son told me, through this blessing, you will overcome all your difficulties.
You will be on your way.
And you will achieve what you want to achieve.
His son told me this.
His son, himself, was a rabbi, [NON-ENGLISH] Klingberg.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I met him in I think Tel Aviv.
No, no.
I didn't-- what I want at that time, I wanted just to survive, first of all to survive.
I didn't have any plans or any ambitions, what to be, and what to-- what to become.
Just to survive.

That God should give me the, the opportunity and the mercy, to be able to see the world-- the world behind me and to go out into the light of freedom and of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the light of freedom again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't formulate any plans for the future, until I was liberated in 1945 by the Russians.
Yes, all the time.
My father was all the time with me.
Same thing, striving to survive, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a-- begging to survive, praying for survive.
I do hope so.
I do hope so, that my prayers and my supplications have helped me, in my achieving the salvation and liberation in 1945.
[SIGHS] You see, the will and the striving to survive was so strong, that if somebody had any trouble-- momentarily, at that moment-- they didn't show it.
Because everybody was focused [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and one-track.
Survival, survival, survival.
Food and survival.
This was the main preoccupation of everyone.
There were some children in the camp.
But in 19-- in May 1944, they were all transported to their death in Auschwitz, from Plaszow camp.
Yes.
This was a day with-- where there was a selection, like.
You know selection?
Selection means people were herded on the big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] square in the, in the, in the camp.
There was a big [NON-ENGLISH] It's called [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was a German doctor.
Everybody had to strip naked.
So he said, here, left was to life, right was to Auschwitz.
And in the mid-- everybody was standing.
And the children, those children who were in, in Plaszow, in, in the camp, they were rounded up by the SS and taken immediately on a, on, on, to the cattle cars and transported to Auschwitz.
No.
Again, the German perfect timing, so that nobody should see.
And there shouldn't be any commotion.
After they were taken.
After they were taken, they found out.
There was a tremendous outcry.
There was a tremendous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sense of tragedy.
But people were so used to tragedy.
They were so used already to, to, to un-- unspeakable atrocities that this was just one more atrocity, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on top of all others.
No, but every morning, there was a bugle call, around 6 o'clock in the morning.
The whole-- there was a-- one Jew, who was-- his name was Rosner-- by the way, Schindler's survivor.
He was, he was a musician.
He knew how to play the trumpet.
So he, he used his-- the bugle to wake up the camp in the morning.
This was the only music I heard.
Where, where, where he lived?
He lived in a villa, on the outskirts of the camp, in a villa, big villa.
Yes.
I saw the villa, yes, of course.
Everybody could see it.
Sometimes, with his concubine.
He had a concubine there who somehow tried to-- I heard that she tried to save Jews, to save the Jews from his, from his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] murderous hands.
No, I didn't see.
From the villa?
No, I didn't see.
I only heard that he was-- that he took people there and-- for the dogs, to be, to, to be attacked by the dogs, for fun.
Again, this was only a short time that we went out to work from Plaszow.
It was about, about two months only.
Because later on, the, the factory was transferred to Plaszow.
Yes, it was very different.
Because it was under the eyes of the SS.
Whereas when we, when we were outside the camp, the Germans did not interfere.
But here, all the time they came looking in and, and, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] enforcing their discipline.
Well, they took people to, to be flogged.
They took people out to be shot.
It was everyday occurrence.
Yes.
I remember that.
But I-- I was so-- I tried not to see it.
I tried to, to, to lower my, my eyes.
Because it was a terrible sight, terrible sight, to see people stripped and, and flogged, 25 times or 50 times.
Because it was a routine expression.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
It was 25 on the, on the behind.
Yes.
Everybody had to count.
From the beginning.
They had to-- they start from the beginning.
Yes.
I heard them count.
[SIGHS]
